message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: BaselineArtifact deep-freezes its entire contents element-by-element, turning a similarity matrix into a recursively tuple-ized copy
DIMENSION: performance & scale
CLAIMED SEVERITY: HIGH
FILE: src/assay_engine/_frozen.py 61-102 (freeze); src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:38-40
DESCRIPTION: BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previous finding, a 5k-embedding baseline that builds an in-memory similarity matrix is materialized at least three times (numpy array -> nested list -> nested tuple). Breaking point: ~3-5k units with any dense O(n^2) content; for raw embedding matrices (n x d) it scales with n*d and a 100k-doc x 768-dim embedding set (77M cells) is hundreds of MB-to-GB of duplicated tuples plus tens of seconds of pure-Python work at artifact construction.
SUGGESTED FIX: Do not deep-freeze large numeric payloads cell-by-cell. The freeze() already has array-like handling (lines 85-94) that canonicalizes objects exposing tobytes()/shape to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) wrappers â€” steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array for bulk numeric content so this fast path is taken, and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays so contents are not recursively tuple-ized. At minimum, document that `contents` must hold buffer-backed arrays (not nested Python lists) for any payload above a few thousand cells.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness â€” fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly â€” if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highâ†’max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only â€” it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK â€” model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER â€” read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" â€” whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) â€” never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named â€” don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
62	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
63	
64	    Mappings â†’ :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples â†’ tuples, sets â†’ frozensets. Known mutable
65	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` â†’ ``bytes``;
66	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` â†’ a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
67	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
68	    construction time* â€” rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
69	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
70	    """
71	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
72	        return value
73	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
74	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
75	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
76	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
77	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
78	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
79	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
80	        return bytes(value)
81	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
82	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
86	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
87	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
88	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
89	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
90	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
91	        )
92	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
93	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
94	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
95	    try:
96	        hash(value)
97	    except TypeError as exc:
98	        raise TypeError(
99	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
100	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
101	        ) from exc
102	    return value
103	
104	
105	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
106	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
107	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
108

1	"""Baseline builder contract + a deterministic artifact wrapper.
2	
3	This module fixes the *shape* of a baseline artifact and the build contract. The engine ships
4	the dataset-agnostic building blocks for a baseline (similarity/distance, descriptive stats,
5	the determinism harness in :mod:`assay_engine.baseline`); the choice-bearing builder that
6	assembles them â€” which embedding model, which clustering, which graph â€” encodes dataset
7	decisions and is supplied by a study's adapter as a :class:`BaselineBuilder` (ADR-0002).
8	
9	Firewall A is enforced here structurally: :meth:`BaselineBuilder.build` accepts a
10	:class:`~assay_engine.methodology.firewalls.ClaimBlindGuard` and never accepts a claims
11	source â€” a baseline simply cannot be handed the claims it must stay blind to.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Mapping, Protocol
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
20	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource
21	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
22	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard
23	
24	
25	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
26	class BaselineArtifact:
27	    """A deterministic, versioned empirical model of the corpus â€” the one privileged object.
28	
29	    Concrete contents (embedding matrices, similarity graphs, cluster assignments, etc.) live
30	    under ``contents`` keyed by builder name. ``determinism`` records the seeds, input hashes,
31	    and model/version strings needed to reproduce it.
32	    """
33	
34	    corpus_fingerprint: str
35	    contents: Mapping[str, Any]
36	    determinism: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
37	
38	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
39	        object.__setattr__(self, "contents", freeze_mapping(self.contents))
40	        object.__setattr__(self, "determinism", freeze_mapping(self.determinism))
41	
42	
43	class BaselineBuilder(Protocol):
44	    """Build a :class:`BaselineArtifact` from a corpus, blind to any external claims."""
45	
46	    def build(
47	        self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource]
48	    ) -> BaselineArtifact:
49	        """Construct the baseline. Must run with ``claim_guard.sealed()`` in force."""
50	        ...
51

content.text: The code is as described. The `freeze()` function does recurse element-by-element through nested lists/tuples (lines 75-76), but it also has a fast path for array-like objects with `tobytes()` (lines 85-94). Let me verify the PoC against the venv.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import time, tracemalloc, sys
sys.path.insert(0, 'src')
from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact

# Build a 2000x2000 matrix as nested python lists
n = 2000
mat = [[float(i*j % 7) for j in range(n)] for i in range(n)]
print('built list matrix', n, 'x', n)

tracemalloc.start()
t0 = time.perf_counter()
art = BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint='x', contents={'sim': mat})
t1 = time.perf_counter()
cur, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
tracemalloc.stop()
print(f'freeze nested-list: {t1-t0:.2f}s, peak +{peak/1e6:.1f}MB')
print('type of frozen sim:', type(art.contents['sim']).__name__)
"

content.text: The nested-list PoC reproduces: 3.61s and +64MB for a 2000x2000 matrix. Now let me verify the numpy fast path works as the suggested fix claims.

    HAS_NP = True
except Exception:

print('numpy available:', HAS_NP)
if HAS_NP:

    print('frozen sim repr head:', repr(art.contents['sim'])[:80])
"

content.text:
The numpy fast path is dramatically faster: 0.006s vs 3.61s (~580x). The +32MB is just the `tobytes()` copy (one copy of the 4M float64 = 32MB), which is O(1) in Python-level work â€” a single descriptor tuple, not 4M nested tuples.

Now the key question: does the engine itself ever produce nested-list contents, or is `contents` populated by adapter-supplied builders (ADR-0002)? Let me check what the engine's own building blocks return.

content.text: Critical finding: `cosine_similarity_matrix` at line 115 returns `list[list[float]]` â€” nested Python lists. This is the engine's own similarity primitive. Let me look at it.

content.content:
69	def _cosine_matrix_pure(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
70	    """Pure-Python reference implementation â€” O(nÂ˛Â·d). Correct but slow; the public function
71	    dispatches to a numpy path at scale when available."""
72	    n = len(rows)
73	    _check_finite(*rows)
74	    norms = [l2_norm(r) for r in rows]
75	    out = [[0.0] * n for _ in range(n)]
76	    for i in range(n):
77	        for j in range(i, n):
78	            if norms[i] == 0.0 or norms[j] == 0.0:
79	                sim = 0.0
80	            elif i == j:
81	                sim = 1.0  # a non-zero vector's self-similarity is exactly 1.0 (no float drift)
82	            else:
83	                sim = _clamp_unit(dot(rows[i], rows[j]) / (norms[i] * norms[j]))
84	            out[i][j] = sim
85	            out[j][i] = sim
86	    return out
87	
88	
89	def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
90	    import numpy as np
91	
92	    x = np.asarray(rows, dtype=float)
93	    if x.ndim != 2:
94	        raise ValueError("rows must be a 2-D collection of equal-length vectors")
95	    if not np.isfinite(x).all():
96	        raise ValueError("vector components must be finite")
97	    norms = np.linalg.norm(x, axis=1)
98	    if not np.isfinite(norms).all():
99	        # finite inputs can still overflow when squared (||x||) on huge-magnitude vectors;
100	        # reject rather than silently produce inf/inf similarities (audit N1; restores parity
101	        # with the pure path, which raises on the same input).
102	        raise ValueError("vector norm overflow â€” component magnitudes are too large")
103	    zero = norms == 0.0
104	    safe = np.where(zero, 1.0, norms)
105	    xn = x / safe[:, None]
106	    m = xn @ xn.T
107	    np.clip(m, -1.0, 1.0, out=m)
108	    if zero.any():  # zero vectors have no direction â†’ similarity 0 to everything
109	        m[zero, :] = 0.0
110	        m[:, zero] = 0.0
111	    np.fill_diagonal(m, np.where(zero, 0.0, 1.0))
112	    return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]
113	
114	
115	def cosine_similarity_matrix(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
116	    """Full symmetric cosine-similarity matrix (diagonal 1.0 for non-zero vectors, 0.0 for zero).
117	
118	    Pure-Python is O(nÂ˛Â·d) and does not scale (a corpus of thousands of embeddings would take
119	    minutes-to-hours); when numpy is available (the optional ``baseline`` extra) this dispatches
120	    to a vectorized path that is orders of magnitude faster. The two paths agree to within
121	    floating-point tolerance (both clamp to [-1, 1]); a study needing byte-identical
122	    reproducibility should pin its environment (the determinism harness records component
123	    versions for exactly this reason).
124	    """
125	    if not rows:
126	        return []
127	    try:
128	        import numpy  # noqa: F401
129	    except ImportError:
130	        return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)
131	    return _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows)
132	
133	
134	def _quantile(sorted_values: list[float], q: float) -> float:
135	    """Linear-interpolation quantile (``q`` in [0, 1]) over already-sorted values."""
136	    if not 0.0 <= q <= 1.0:
137	        raise ValueError(f"quantile q must be in [0, 1]; got {q}")
138	    n = len(sorted_values)

content.text:
This confirms an important point: the engine's own similarity primitive `cosine_similarity_matrix` returns `list[list[float]]` (nested Python lists), even on the numpy path (line 112 converts back to lists). So the natural workflow â€” build matrix with the engine primitive, stuff into `contents` â€” DOES hit the slow nested-list freeze path. The suggested fix to "steer builders toward numpy" is somewhat undermined by the engine's own primitive returning lists.

However, let me assess the actual production impact realistically. The key questions: (1) is this on a hot path, (2) is the magnitude truly a production blocker, (3) is the hard constraint that the engine doesn't import numpy relevant.

Let me check the determinism builder that actually constructs the artifact in-engine.

content.content:
95	) -> BaselineArtifact:
96	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
97	
98	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, â€¦).
99	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
100	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
101	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
102	    so it is reproducible.
103	    """
104	    corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
105	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
106	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key â€” rename the extra input (#B7)")
107	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
108	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
109	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
110	
111	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
112	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
113	
114	    if seed is None:
115	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
116	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
117	
118	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
119	    return BaselineArtifact(
120	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
121	    )

content.text: Now let me measure the realistic end-to-end scenario described in the finding: the engine builds a similarity matrix (returns nested lists), then it's frozen. Let me confirm the combined cost matches the claim and check whether there's any guard/size limit.

content.text:
All confirmed. Summary of my independent verification:

**Reproduced:**
- Freezing a 2000x2000 nested-list matrix into `BaselineArtifact.contents`: 3.61s, +64MB (matches the claimed 3.29s/64MB).
- The numpy fast path (lines 85-94) reduces this to 0.006s / +32MB (one `tobytes()` copy + a single descriptor tuple) â€” ~580x faster.
- `array.array('d', ...)` also takes the fast path: 0.007s / +32MB.

**Confirmed the mechanism and the real risk amplifier:** The engine's own primitive `cosine_similarity_matrix` (primitives.py:115) returns `list[list[float]]` â€” and even its numpy path (line 112) converts the result back to nested Python lists. So a builder that uses the engine's own building block and stores the result in `contents` (the documented natural workflow) hits the slow path, not the fast path. The suggested fix ("steer builders toward numpy/array") is partially undermined by the engine itself emitting lists.

**Confirmed gaps:** No size guard/warning in `freeze()`, and no documentation in toolkit.py or docs/ that `contents` should hold buffer-backed arrays rather than nested lists for large payloads.

**Severity recalibration â€” I downgrade HIGH to MEDIUM:**
- The expensive path is at artifact *construction* (once per baseline build), not a per-query hot loop. A baseline is a deterministic, build-once object.
- A clean, documented fast path already exists (numpy ndarray / array.array â†’ O(1) descriptor). This is a usage/contract gap, not a missing capability.
- The 3.6s/64MB cost is real but bounded; the catastrophic "GB / tens of seconds" figure requires a 100k-doc dense embedding stored as raw nested lists, which is an extreme/avoidable configuration.
- The hard constraint that the engine never imports numpy (ADR-0006, lazy heavy backends) means the engine *can't* unconditionally store arrays; it must accept user contents as-is. So the right remediation is a documented contract + optional size warning, not a forced rewrite â€” consistent with a MEDIUM-grade hardening item rather than a release blocker.

It is a genuine production-readiness defect (real perf/memory cliff with no guardrail or guidance), but not HIGH.

input.reasoning:
The finding is technically accurate and I reproduced it against the venv. freeze() (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:75-76) recurses element-by-element through lists/tuples, converting an n*m nested-list matrix into a parallel nested-tuple copy and calling hash() on every leaf. BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:38-40) runs freeze_mapping over contents, so any builder stuffing a similarity/embedding matrix as nested Python lists pays this cost. PoC: a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) nested-list matrix froze in 3.61s with +64MB peak; the numpy-array fast path (lines 85-94, which canonicalizes tobytes()/shape-bearing objects to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor) froze the same data in 0.006s/+32MB, ~580x faster; array.array('d') also hits the fast path at 0.007s/+32MB. The risk is amplified by the engine's OWN primitive cosine_similarity_matrix (src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:115) returning list[list[float]] â€” even the numpy path (line 112) converts back to nested lists â€” so the natural workflow (use engine primitive, store in contents) hits the slow path, partially undermining the 'steer toward numpy' fix. There is no size guard/warning in freeze() and no documentation in toolkit.py or docs/ stating that contents should be buffer-backed for large payloads.

I downgrade the claimed HIGH to MEDIUM: (1) the cost is at one-time artifact construction, not a per-query hot loop â€” a baseline is a build-once deterministic object; (2) a clean documented-capable fast path already exists (numpy ndarray / array.array â†’ O(1) descriptor), so this is a contract/usage gap, not a missing capability; (3) the catastrophic GB/tens-of-seconds figure requires an extreme, avoidable configuration (100k-doc dense embeddings stored as raw nested lists); (4) the hard constraint that the engine must not import numpy (ADR-0006 lazy heavy backends) means the engine cannot unconditionally store arrays and must accept user contents as-is â€” the correct remediation is a documented contents-type contract plus an optional size-based warning, consistent with a MEDIUM hardening item rather than a release blocker. It is a genuine production-readiness defect (real perf/memory cliff with no guardrail or guidance), just not HIGH.
input.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (freeze lines 61-102, list/tuple recursion at 75-76, array fast path at 85-94) and src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py (BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ 38-40). Read src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:69-131 â€” cosine_similarity_matrix returns list[list[float]], numpy path line 112 converts back to lists. Read determinism.py:95-121 (build_baseline passes contents straight to BaselineArtifact). PoC runs against /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv: nested-list 2000x2000 = 3.61s/+64MB; numpy ndarray same size = 0.006s/+32MB; array.array(4M doubles) = 0.007s/+32MB. grep confirmed no size/limit/warn guard in _frozen.py and no 'contents' content-type guidance in docs/.

content.text: Verification complete. I confirmed the finding is real (reproduced 3.61s/+64MB for a 2000x2000 nested-list matrix vs 0.006s for the numpy fast path), and recalibrated severity from HIGH to MEDIUM since it's a one-time construction cost on an avoidable usage pattern with an existing fast path, not a hot-loop blocker. Key amplifier worth noting: the engine's own `cosine_similarity_matrix` at `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:115` returns nested lists, so the natural workflow lands on the slow path.
